750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}

813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}

818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}

865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}

897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.

994	% $$

996	% $$

1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.

1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[

1041	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift

24	
25	\section{Setup and motivation}
26	
27	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
28	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
29	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
30	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
31	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
33	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
34	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
35	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
36	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
37	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
38	
39	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
40	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
41	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
42	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
43	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
44	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
45	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
46	\[
47	  \begin{array}{ccc}
48	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
49	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
50	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
51	  \end{array}
52	\]
53	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
54	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
56	
57	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
58	
59	\begin{definition}
60	
61	\leanok
62	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
63	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
64	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
65	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
66	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
67	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
68	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
69	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
70	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
71	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
72	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
73	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
74	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
75	  % the unit ideal, see
76	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
77	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
78	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
79	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
80	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
81	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
82	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
83	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
84	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
85	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
86	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
87	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
88	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
89	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
90	  \[
91	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
92	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
93	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
94	  \]
95	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
96	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
97	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
98	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
99	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
100	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
101	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
102	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
103	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
104	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
105	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
106	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
107	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
108	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
109	\end{definition}
110	
111	\begin{definition}
112	
113	\leanok
114	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
115	  \label{def:cech_complex}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
117	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
118	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
119	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
120	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
121	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
122	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
123	  % is identified with the complex
124	  % $$
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
126	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
127	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
128	  % \ldots
129	  % $$
130	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
131	  % \begin{equation}
132	  % \label{equation-extended}
133	  % 0 \to
134	  % M \to
135	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
136	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
137	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
138	  % \ldots
139	  % \end{equation}
140	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
141	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
142	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
143	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
144	  vanishing).}
145	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
146	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
147	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
148	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
149	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
150	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
151	  in degree \(p\) it is
152	  \[
153	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
154	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
155	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
156	  \]
157	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
158	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
159	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
160	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
161	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
162	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
163	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
164	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
165	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
166	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
167	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
168	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
169	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
170	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
171	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
172	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
176	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
177	
178	It is convenient to factor the construction of
179	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
180	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
181	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
182	
183	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
184	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
185	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
186	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
187	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
188	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
189	\[
190	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
191	    \;=\;
192	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
193	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
194	\]
195	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
196	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
197	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
198	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
199	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
200	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
201	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
202	
203	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
204	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
205	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
206	\[
207	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
208	  \qquad
209	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
210	\]
211	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
212	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
213	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
214	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
215	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
216	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
217	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
218	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
219	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
220	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
221	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
222	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
223	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
224	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
225	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
226	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
227	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
228	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
229	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
230	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
231	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
232	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
233	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
234	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
235	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
236	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
237	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
238	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
239	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
240	
241	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
242	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
243	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
244	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
245	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
246	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
247	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
248	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
249	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
250	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
251	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
252	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
253	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
254	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
255	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
256	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
257	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
258	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
259	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
260	
261	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
262	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
263	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
264	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
265	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
266	
267	\begin{definition}\leanok
268	[Arrow of an affine cover]
269	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
270	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
271	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
272	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
273	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
274	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
275	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
276	  apply.
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
280	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
282	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
283	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
284	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
285	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
286	  \[
287	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
288	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
289	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
290	  \]
291	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
292	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
293	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
294	\end{definition}
295	
296	\begin{definition}\leanok
297	[Push--pull object map]
298	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
300	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme

content:
# Strategy

## Goal

Prove `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (`lem:cech_computes_cohomology`),
the protected, frozen-signature target: for `f : X ⟶ S` separated and quasi-compact, `F`
quasi-coherent, `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X`, an isomorphism in the weak existence
form `Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F)` (under
`[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`), with `higherDirectImage f i F =
((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F`. End-state: zero inline `sorry` in the cone, zero
project axioms, kernel-only axioms.

## Phases & estimations

| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P5a-resolution `cechAugmented_exact` (augmented Čech complex resolves F) | ACTIVE (stubs) | ~4–8 | ~250–450 | Sub-brick A in `CechSectionIdentification.lean`: Stub 3 DONE; Stubs 5/6 re-spec'd to the AUGMENTED complex `D'_aug` (degree-0 augmentation node = sheaf equalizer); foundation Stub 1 = missing-Mathlib scheme coproduct/fibre-product distribution → Stub 2/4 chained behind it. | Stub 1 is the bottleneck — a from-scratch extensive-category/pullback-stability build (analogist-scoped); not a one-session item. Estimate WIDENED past the bare ~3–5 to acknowledge the 01I8-comparable risk (that route overran ~7×). Shared with dead `CechAcyclic.affine`. |
| P5a-consumer `higherDirectImage_openImmersion_comp` (affine open immersions are f_*-acyclic) | ACTIVE (seed) | ~3–6 | ~290–440 | **Need#2 infra DONE** (7 axiom-clean decls iter-056). Active residual = the change-of-ring **seed** `htilde` (route B1: base-change to `S=Γ(V)` via `M⊗_R S`, per-σ localization from `Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.BaseChange`, co-located in `CechAcyclic.lean`). Then **Need#1** whole-scheme `U≅SpecΓU` Ext transport (`Scheme.isoSpec`+`Ext.mapExactFunctor`). | seed is general affine `V` NOT `D(f)` ⟹ done `_of_localizationAway` tool does not apply; ~230–320 LOC, comparable to QcohTildeSections. Naive open-subscheme `j⁻¹V≅SpecΓ(j⁻¹V)` Ext transport REJECTED (restriction-injectives WALL). |
| P5b comparison assembly | BLOCKED | ~2–3 | ~150–300 | P4 engine + P5a resolution + termwise `f_*`-acyclicity + EnoughInjectives connector. | Final Route-A assembly of the protected goal `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (line-780 sorry). Gated on P5a. |

## Completed

| Phase | Iters (done@ · used) | LOC | Files | Key results | Reusable techniques | Pitfalls |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 push–pull functor laws | 002 · 2 | ~120 | `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` | `pushPullMap_comp` closing push–pull functoriality | object-form align `simp [Functor.comp_obj]` before `reassoc_of%`; `rawPushPullMap`+`subst`+pentagon | `conjugateEquiv_comp` mate route INFEASIBLE (kernel `whnf` blow-up) |
| P2 `CechNerve`/`CechComplex` | 002 · 1 | ~60 | `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` | `pushPullFunctor`, `coverCechNerve(Aug)`, `CechNerve`, `CechComplex` axiom-clean | `Over.lift`+`.rightOp`+`CosimplicialObject.Augmented.whiskeringObj`; terminal augmentation | none |
| P4 acyclic-resolution lemma (Leray, 015E) | 009 · 6 | ~965 | `AcyclicResolution.lean` | `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`, `rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic`, horseshoe `ofShortExact`, dimension shift — axiom-clean | decompose-then-build; two-step `cokernel.mapIso`; `Nat.rec` staircase off `stairGen` | `ShortComplex.mapCyclesIso` WRONG for left-exact functor; `← G.map_comp` silently fails by a mapped-complex term |
| P3 standard-cover Čech vanishing (section form, tilde) | 022 · ~14 | ~1200 | `CechAcyclic.lean` | `sectionCech_affine_vanishing` + `sectionCech_homology_exact` (IsZero homology p≥1) for `F=~M`; L1 tilde-bridge + L3 combinatorial core | accessors DEFEQ (`rfl`); abstract section maps (`set;clear_value`) before `IsLocalizedModule.ext`; target ABSOLUTE section complex | residual qcoh `F≅~ΓF` (01I8) deferred to 02KG consumer; old relative `affine` sorry superseded |
| P3b Čech↔derived bridge bricks | 025 · ~9 | ~1500 | `PresheafCech`,`FreePresheafComplex`,`CechBridge` | `cechFreeComplex_quasiIso` (free resolution of `O_𝒰`), `ses_cech_h1`, `injective_cech_acyclic` (pos-degree Čech vanishing for injectives) — axiom-clean | op-transport via `opFunctor`+`preadditiveYoneda`; `erw`+`.trans` for defeq-carrier mismatch; Route-B arrow-iso transfer | `maxHeartbeats 2000000` needed; LSP-staleness (trust only `lake env lean`) |
| Absolute-cohomology Form B + 01EO general basis criterion | 028 · 3 | ~600 | `AbsoluteCohomology`,`CechToCohomology` | `jShriekOU`=sheafify(free(yoneda U)); H⁰≅Γ (+naturality); 01EO chain L1–L4+top (`cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis`); `BasisCovSystem`/`HasVanishingHigherCech` cover-system encoding | Form B kills restriction-preserves-injectives (injective in 2nd Ext arg); cover-local presheaf form; `attribute [local instance] hasExtModules`; AB4* `Epi(Pi.map)` elementwise | `[EnoughInjectives X.Modules]` absent in Mathlib → carried as explicit hyp (P5a convention); 3 first-attempt iters, no churn |
| 01I8 `F≅~(ΓF)` via section-localization (Route B) | 048 · ~14 (040→048) | ~900 | `QcohTildeSections`,`QcohRestrictBasicOpen` | KEYSTONE `qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule` (ρ_f localizes Γ); Route-B assembly `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_quasicoherent` ⟹ **unconditional `qcoh_iso_tilde_sections`**; tile chain B0–B4 + `qcoh_section_equalizer` + `isLocalizedModule_of_exact` | sheaf-axiom equalizer (NOT span-cover, which was circular); bundled `restrictScalars`-carrier for two-action tile descent; `change`/defeq for the `↑R`-Semiring diamond; basis-check via `IsCoverDense.iso_of_restrict_iso`+`specBasicOpen` | span-cover descent on global sections is CIRCULAR; tile lemma is NOT `restrict_obj`-rfl; `IsLocalizing`/`isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizing` absent; over-budget (~14 iters vs est ~2) — the single hardest sub-route |
| 02KG affine Serre vanishing (cover-system + tops) | 052 · ~4 (049→052) | ~1100 | `AffineSerreVanishing`,`CechAcyclic` | unconditional `affine_serre_vanishing` + `affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh`; residual `sectionCech_homology_exact_of_localizationAway` (route-B change-of-ring); `affineCoverSystem : BasisCovSystem` | the `private SectionCech*` core is `{R}[CommRing R]`-polymorphic → re-instantiate `dDiff_exact` over `R_f` at module level (no sheaf infra); discharge tops via `_of_tildeVanishing` reshaper | the residual covers a PROPER `D(f)` (cover spans `√f`, not `R`); plan-validate stop-marker/noop traps cost iters 050/051 |

## Routes

### Route A — acyclic-resolution comparison (CHOSEN)
The augmented Čech complex `0 → F → C⁰ → C¹ → ⋯` on `X` (`Cᵖ = ∏ (j_s)_*(F|_{U_s})`) is
(i) a resolution of `F` and (ii) termwise `(pushforward f)`-acyclic. The P4 abstract lemma
gives `Hⁱ(f_* C•) ≅ Rⁱf_* F` directly — ONE abstract lemma, NO spectral sequences. Acyclicity
input (ii) reduces to affine Serre vanishing `H^q(affine, qcoh)=0`, supplied by the P3b bridge.

### Route SS — two spectral sequences (REJECTED)
Both absent from Mathlib (multi-thousand-LOC); rests on the same `injective_cech_acyclic` brick as A.
(Named "Route SS" to avoid collision with the 01I8-internal "Route B" section-localization route below.)

### The acyclicity bridge (torsor-free, load-bearing)
Route A's acyclicity reduces to affine Serre vanishing `H^q(Spec A, qcoh)=0` (02KG). The honest
non-circular route is the minimal Čech↔derived bridge: (1) `injective_cech_acyclic`, (2)
`ses_cech_h1`, (3) the dimension-shift `cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis` (01EO) consuming the
standard-cover Čech vanishing of P3 as its condition (3). All three bricks of (1)+(2) are done;
01EO is the next target. This breaks the circular regress: P3 produces standard-cover Čech
vanishing; the bridge lifts it to affine sheaf vanishing without ever using affine vanishing.

### `cechAugmented_exact` — sections/sheafification route (P5a resolution input)
The augmented Čech complex is a resolution of `F` (ANY `O_X`-module, ANY open cover; qcoh/affineness
are needed only by the downstream f_*-acyclicity, not by this exactness). Route: reflect `IsZero(Hᵖ)`
through the faithful additive `SheafOfModules.toSheaf` (3-line `IsZero.iff_id_eq_zero`+`map_injective`
helper — `reflects_exact_of_faithful` needs `[Abelian]`, avoid); the homology SHEAF = sheafification of
the presheaf homology (`homologyIsoSheafify`, built); the presheaf homology is **locally zero on the
basis `{V ⊆ some Uᵢ}`** because the restricted cover `{Uₛ∩V}` then has a member `=V`, giving the
cover-agnostic prepend-`i_fix` contracting homotopy (`CombinatorialCech.combHomotopy`/`combHomotopy_spec`,
F-valued objectwise analogue), so its sheafification vanishes via `sheafify_kills_locally_zero` + the
`toSheaf∘sheafify ≅ presheafToSheaf∘forget` square (`PresheafOfModules.sheafificationCompToSheaf`).
DEAD ends: stalk functor (no `SheafOfModules.stalk`); the tilde/standard-cover vanishing as the local
discharger (wrong cover — that's the 02KG tool); the naive "section complex exact over each affine V"
(CIRCULAR = `Ȟᵖ(V,·)`≠0). Analogist: `analogies/tosheaf-reflect.md`.
The local discharge's only hard piece (**Sub-brick A**, the per-degree section identification
`Γ(V,pushPullObj F Yₚ)≅∏_σ Γ(U_σ∩V,F)`) is decomposed into the new shared
`CechSectionIdentification.lean`: ONE new-infra leaf (coproduct→product of module sheaves) + four
off-the-shelf pieces + differential via `sectionCech_objD_apply` + Sub-brick-B contractibility via the
de-privatized `Dependent` engine (prepend `i_fix`, identity since `V≤coverOpen i`). Analogist:
`analogies/subbrickA.md`.

### Absolute cohomology realization — Ext of the corepresenting object (Form B)
`H^p(U, F) := Ext^p_{X.Modules}(jShriekOU U, F)` where `jShriekOU U := sheafify(free(yoneda U))`
is the corepresenting object of `F ↦ Γ(U,F) = F(U)` (`= j_!O_U` up to iso), built from the
already-shipped `freeYonedaHomEquiv` + `sheafificationAdjunction` (~50–80 LOC). The three facts
01EO consumes are then off-the-shelf with the SES staying in `X.Modules`: injective vanishing
`Ext.eq_zero_of_injective` (injective `I` as the 2nd arg — no restriction taken), covariant LES
`Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁/₂/₃` at fixed 1st arg `jShriekOU U`, and `H⁰ ≅ Γ(U,F)` via the
corepresentability chain. The discarded alternatives: Form A `Ext(O_U, F|_U)` needs
restriction-preserves-injectives (≈ a 200–500 LOC `j_!` functor); `Functor.rightDerived` has
free injective vanishing but no packaged LES; `Sheaf.H` is absent. Reversal signal: Ext
universe/smallness pain over `SheafOfModules` → fall back to Route γ (Čech colimit), never `Sheaf.H`.

## Open strategic questions

- **EnoughInjectives connector (cone obligation).** The frozen target provides
  `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]` but the 01EO/02KG cone carries the stronger
  `[EnoughInjectives X.Modules]`. Connector `HasInjectiveResolutions C → EnoughInjectives C` is TRUE,
  ~6 LOC (deg-0 mono into `I.cocomplex.X 0` via `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.instMonoFNatι`).
  Build + register at the P5b assembly lane — DISSOLVES the "EnoughInjectives absent in Mathlib" worry.
- P5a last-mile bridge `Hᵏ((f_*I^•)(V)) = Ext^k(jShriek(f⁻¹V), G)`: Ext-by-injective-resolution-of-2nd-arg
  backing CONFIRMED present (`InjectiveResolution.extEquivCohomologyClass`). Due when P5a consumers dispatch.
- Chapter cleanup (non-blocking): `lem:qcoh_localized_sections` is circular by the old span-cover
  mechanism but DORMANT (no DAG path to the main theorem); re-route or delete in a future writer pass.

## Mathlib gaps & new material

Gaps to fill (project-side, Mathlib lacks):
- Standard-cover Čech complex exactness `0→M→∏M_{f_i}→⋯` (P3) — done via L1+L3 around `exact_of_isLocalized_span`.
- Presheaf-level Čech machinery for `O_X`-modules (P3b free/section complexes, `injective_cech_acyclic`,
  `ses_cech_h1`) — done. Dropped as off-critical-path: presheaf enough-injectives + δ-functor universality.
- Absolute module-valued `Hⁿ(U,F)` = `Ext^p(jShriekOU U, F)` via `Abelian.Ext` (Form B); corepresenting
  object `jShriekOU = sheafify(free(yoneda U))`. NOT a bespoke `j_!` functor (that is absent, 200–500 LOC).
- Relative/open-immersion affine vanishing `R^q(jₛ)_*=0` (P5a-consumer). Sound route = Need#2
  enlarge `affineCoverSystem` basis to all affine opens (general-affine-open Serre vanishing,
  no restriction functor) + Need#1 whole-scheme `U≅SpecΓU` Ext transport. The open-subscheme
  `j⁻¹V≅SpecΓ(j⁻¹V)` transport is REJECTED (= restriction-preserves-injectives wall, analogist iter-056).
  CLARIFICATION (avoid re-deriving the wall): the Ext that Need#1 transports along `U≅SpecΓU` is the
  STANDALONE `Ext_{U.Modules}` (cohomology of `U` as its own scheme, via an EQUIVALENCE of module
  categories → `Ext.mapExactFunctor`), NOT the ambient `Ext_{X.Modules}(jShriekOU V,-)`; the
  ambient↔concrete bridge is the 01EO Čech comparison + the section-level seed `htilde`, never an
  ambient→restricted Ext map. Need#2's seed `htilde` is built at the SECTION (module) level for this reason.
- EnoughInjectives connector `HasInjectiveResolutions C → EnoughInjectives C` (~6 LOC, instance) — see
  Open questions; makes the 01EO/02KG cone type-check against the frozen target's weaker hypothesis.
- `cechAugmented_exact` (P5a resolution input): the toSheaf→`homologyIsoSheafify`→`sheafify_kills_locally_zero`
  bridge is DONE; residual = Sub-brick A. ONE new sheaf-infra leaf: indexed coproduct→product of module
  sheaves `(q_p)_*(q_p)^*F ≅ ∏_σ (j_σ)_*(j_σ)^*F` (Mathlib has only binary / structure-sheaf —
  `coprodPresheafObjIso`, `isProductOfDisjoint`). Everything else off-the-shelf / reuse. Lives in new
  `CechSectionIdentification.lean`. ~200–350 LOC.

New project material:

- `CechNerve`/`CechComplex`; `AcyclicResolution.lean` (P4); P3b free-presheaf + section Čech complexes,

  injective-vanishing + LES wrappers → 01EO `cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis` → 02KG `affine_serre_vanishing`.
- comparison assembly `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` via Route A.

301	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
302	  \[
303	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
304	  \]
305	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
306	\end{definition}
307	
308	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
309	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
311	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
312	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
313	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
314	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
315	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
316	  \[
317	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
318	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
319	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
320	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
321	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
322	      \;=\;
323	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
324	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
325	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
326	  \]
327	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
328	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
329	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
330	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
331	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
332	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
333	\end{definition}
334	
335	\begin{lemma}
336	
337	\leanok
338	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
339	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
341	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
342	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
343	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	
348	\leanok
349	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
350	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
351	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
352	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
353	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
354	\end{proof}
355	
356	\begin{lemma}
357	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
358	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
359	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
360	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
361	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
362	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
363	  \[
364	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
365	  \]
366	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
367	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
368	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
369	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
370	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
371	\end{lemma}
372	
373	\begin{proof}
374	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
375	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
376	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
377	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
378	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
379	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
380	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
381	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
382	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
383	\end{proof}
384	
385	\begin{lemma}
386	
387	\leanok
388	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
389	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
390	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
391	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
392	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
393	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
394	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
395	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
396	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
397	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
398	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
399	  \[
400	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
401	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
402	    \;=\;
403	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
404	  \]
405	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
406	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
407	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
408	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
409	\end{lemma}
410	
411	\begin{proof}
412	
413	\leanok
414	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
415	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
416	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
417	\end{proof}
418	
419	\begin{lemma}
420	
421	\leanok
422	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
423	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
425	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
426	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
427	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
428	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
429	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
430	  collapses to the transport-light form
431	  \[
432	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
433	      \;\circ\; e_2
434	    \;=\;
435	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
436	      \;\circ\; e_3,
437	  \]
438	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
439	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
440	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
441	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
442	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
443	  composition (pentagon) law.
444	\end{lemma}
445	
446	\begin{proof}
447	
448	\leanok
449	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
450	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
451	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
452	\end{proof}
453	
454	\begin{definition}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[Push--pull functor]
458	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
460	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
461	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
462	  \[
463	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
464	  \]
465	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
466	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
467	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
468	  laws established above: the identity law
469	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
470	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
471	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
472	\end{definition}
473	
474	\begin{definition}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
478	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
480	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
481	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
482	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
483	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
484	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
485	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
486	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
487	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
488	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
489	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
490	\end{definition}
491	
492	\begin{definition}\leanok
493	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
494	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
495	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
496	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
497	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
498	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
499	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
500	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
501	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
502	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
503	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
504	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
505	\end{definition}
506	
507	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
508	
509	\begin{definition}
510	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
511	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
513	  \mathlibok
514	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
515	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
516	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
517	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
518	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
519	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
520	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
521	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
522	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
523	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
524	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
525	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
526	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
527	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
528	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
529	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
530	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
531	\end{definition}
532	
533	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
534	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
535	
536	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
537	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
538	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
539	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
540	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
541	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
542	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
543	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
544	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
545	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
546	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
548	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
549	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
550	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
551	
552	\begin{definition}
553	
554	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
555	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
557	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
558	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
559	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
560	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
561	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
562	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
563	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
564	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
565	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
566	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
567	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
568	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
569	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
570	  % as an $R_f$-module.
571	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
572	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
573	  % are the maps
574	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
575	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
576	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
577	  items (4)--(5).}
578	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
579	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
580	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
581	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
582	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
583	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
584	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
585	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
586	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
587	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
588	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
589	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
590	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
591	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
592	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
593	
594	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
595	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
596	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
597	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
598	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
599	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
600	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for

851	  \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\)
852	  (\(\operatorname{qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}\)), hence canonically isomorphic over
853	  \(M\). Assembling the coordinatewise isomorphisms after the product equivalence gives
854	  the stated identification.
855	\end{proof}

857	\begin{lemma}
858	[Abstract cosimplicial unfold of the section {\v C}ech differential]
859	  \label{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}

861	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechFaceRestr}
862	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
863	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
864	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open cover. For a multi-index
865	  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(q+2) \to I\) and a face index
866	  \(i \in \mathrm{Fin}(q+2)\), write
867	  \[
868	    \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i) :
869	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\Bigr)
870	      \;\longrightarrow\;
871	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)}\Bigr)
872	  \]
873	  for the \(i\)-th presheaf face restriction --- the value of \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
874	  inclusion of opens
875	  \(\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)} \subseteq \bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\)
876	  obtained from the order-preserving coface \(d_i\) of the simplex category. Then,
877	  read through the product equivalence
878	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\) of
879	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the underlying group action of the
880	  degree-\(q\) differential
881	  \(\operatorname{objD} q\) of the alternating-coface-map complex
882	  \(\operatorname{AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex}
883	  (\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\,\mathcal{U}\,\mathcal{F})\) is the alternating
884	  sum of the face restrictions applied to the deleted-face coordinates:
885	  \[
886	    \bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q+1}\,(\operatorname{objD} q\, t)\bigr)_\sigma
887	      \;=\;
888	      \sum_{i = 0}^{q+1} (-1)^i \,
889	        \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
890	          \Bigl(\bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q}\, t\bigr)_{\sigma \circ d_i}\Bigr).
891	  \]
892	  This is \emph{purely cosimplicial}: it records the shape of the section {\v C}ech
893	  differential as an alternating sum of presheaf restrictions, with \emph{no}
894	  sheaf-theoretic or localisation identification of those restrictions. It is the
895	  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
896	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} matches once the tilde-bridge
897	  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match} is supplied.
898	\end{lemma}
899	
900	\begin{proof}

8498	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
8499	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.

8501	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
8502	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
8503	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
8504	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
8505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
8506	
8507	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
8508	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
8509	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
8510	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
8511	
8512	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
8513	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
8514	  sheafification of
8515	  \[

8517	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
8518	  \]
8519	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
8520	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
8521	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
8522	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
8523	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
8524	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing

8531	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison

8537	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)

8545	
8546	  \emph{(1) The cohomology-presheaf identification.} The objectwise homology in degree
8547	  \(n\) of the pushed-forward resolution complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\), evaluated at an open
8548	  \(V\), is identified with the absolute cohomology presheaf
8549	  \(V \mapsto H^n(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}) = \operatorname{Ext}^n\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)},
8550	  \mathcal{G}\bigr)\). Concretely: \(\operatorname{Ext}^n(X, Y)\) over an injective resolution of
8551	  the second argument \(Y\) is identified with the \(n\)-th cohomology of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)
8552	  cochain complex \(H^n\bigl(\operatorname{Hom}(X[0], \mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)\), via the composite
8553	  of the Ext-to-cohomology-class equivalence and the cohomology-class-to-homology equivalence of the
8554	  Hom complex (the equivalences of Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homcomplex_mathlib}). Taking \(X = j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)}\) and \(Y =
8555	  \mathcal{G}\), the degree-\(k\) Hom group \(\operatorname{Hom}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)},
8556	  \mathcal{I}^k)\) is identified with \(\Gamma(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{I}^k) = (f_*\mathcal{I}^k)(V)\) by

8562	  lemma is needed, since both compute the same homology over the same injective resolution and meet at
8563	  corepresentability.
8564	
8565	  \emph{(2) Serre vanishing for a general affine open.} The Serre vanishing of
8566	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} is pinned to the \emph{top} open \(\top\) of a spectrum
8567	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\): it kills \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_\top, -)\) for \(q \geq 1\).
8568	  The cohomology that actually occurs here is over a \emph{general} affine open --- the preimage
8569	  \(j^{-1}(V)\) (respectively \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\)) of an affine open \(V\) of \(S\) that may straddle
8570	  the boundary of \(j(U)\), so \(j^{-1}(V)\) is a general, possibly non-distinguished, affine open of
8571	  \(U\). The residual is therefore \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_{j^{-1}(V)}, \mathcal{H}) = 0\)
8572	  over the abstract affine scheme \(U\), for \(q \geq 1\). We discharge it by splitting it into two
8573	  independent transports.

8575	  \emph{(2a) The residual lives over an affine scheme.} The immersion \(j\) is an affine morphism:
8576	  for \(U\) affine and \(X\) separated,

8578	  open of \(U\). In particular \(j^{-1}(V) = U \cap V\) (and likewise \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\)) is an
8579	  affine open of the affine scheme \(U\). After the reduction to the right-derived sections functor
8580	  --- by the presheaf description (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) and the
8581	  corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso}, upgraded to right derived
8582	  functors by Lemma~\ref{lem:rightDerivedNatIso} --- the residual reads

8584	  vanishing in the module category of the abstract affine scheme \(U\), for \(q \geq 1\).

8586	  \emph{(2b) Transport along the whole-scheme spectrum isomorphism (Need~\#1).} The abstract affine
8587	  scheme \(U\) carries the canonical \emph{whole-scheme} isomorphism
8588	  \(U \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\) (\(\operatorname{Scheme.isoSpec}\),
8589	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib}), a genuine isomorphism of schemes because \(U\) is affine.

8980	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} over the base ring \(S\), with module \(M \otimes_R S\) and
8981	  the spanning family \(\bar g : \iota \to S\); spanning is exactly the affineness of the cover's union,

8990	  of the localisation comparison.

9085	  M_{g_\sigma}\) (Tag 01HV item (4), \(g_\sigma = \prod_k g_{\sigma k}\)). We identify it, degreewise, with
9086	  the standard-cover (full-span) section {\v C}ech complex over \(\operatorname{Spec} S\) of
9087	  \(\widetilde{M \otimes_R S}\), whose degree-\(p\) term is \(\prod_\sigma (M \otimes_R S)_{\bar g_\sigma}\).
9088	  The per-\(\sigma\) comparison \(M_{g_\sigma} \cong (M \otimes_R S)_{\bar g_\sigma}\) is a localised
9089	  base-change isomorphism: \(\Gamma(D(g_\sigma)) = R_{g_\sigma}\) is simultaneously the localisation of \(R\)
9090	  away from \(g_\sigma\) and the localisation of \(S\) away from \(\bar g_\sigma\) (since
9091	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\operatorname{basicOpen}\bar g_\sigma = D(g_\sigma)\)), and \((M \otimes_R
9092	  S)_{\bar g_\sigma}\) is the base change of \(M\) along the composite \(R \to S \to S_{\bar g_\sigma}\;\)
9093	  (transitivity of base change for localised modules). These comparisons are the vertical maps of an
9094	  isomorphism of cochain complexes, commuting with the alternating-sum cofaces by naturality of the
9095	  localisation comparison.
9096	
9097	  \emph{Conclusion.} Over \(S\) the family \(\bar g\) spans the unit ideal, so the full-span standard-cover
9098	  vanishing --- the polymorphic core of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, re-instantiated over \(S\) with
9099	  the module \(M \otimes_R S\), exactly as it supplies the quasi-coherent seed of
9100	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh} on a whole spectrum --- gives positive-degree exactness of the
9101	  \(\operatorname{Spec} S\) complex. This change-of-base computation and its transport back along the
9102	  per-\(\sigma\) base-change isomorphisms \(M_{g_\sigma} \cong (M \otimes_R S)_{\bar g_\sigma}\)
9103	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_baseChange_away}) is exactly
9104	  Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionCech_homology_exact_of_affineCover}, whose conclusion is the asserted vanishing
9105	  \(\check{H}^p(\{D(g_i)\}, \widetilde{M}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
9106	\end{proof}

9247	\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso commutes with the free functor]
9248	  \label{lem:pushforward_commutes_free}
9249	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_commutes_free}
9250	  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
9251	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou}
9252	  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and write
9253	  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi : X.\mathrm{Modules} \simeq Y.\mathrm{Modules}\)
9254	  for the induced equivalence of module categories, with \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) the corresponding
9255	  pushforward on presheaves of modules along the homeomorphism underlying \(\varphi\). Then for every
9256	  presheaf of modules \(P\) on \(X\) there is a natural isomorphism
9257	  \[
9258	    \Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(\operatorname{free} P\bigr)
9259	      \;\cong\;
9260	    \operatorname{free}\bigl(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}} P\bigr),
9261	  \]
9262	  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor of Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}.
9263	\end{lemma}
9264	\begin{proof}
9265	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou}
9266	  The free-module functor is the left adjoint of the forgetful functor from presheaves of modules to
9267	  presheaves of abelian groups, and pushforward along the isomorphism \(\varphi\) is itself an
9268	  equivalence that commutes with the forgetful functors --- it relabels opens along the underlying
9269	  homeomorphism and leaves the underlying abelian-group data untouched. A left adjoint composed with an
9270	  equivalence is again a left adjoint, now to the conjugate forgetful functor; the mate of the identity
9271	  comparison between the two forgetful functors is the asserted natural isomorphism
9272	  \(\Phi(\operatorname{free} P) \cong \operatorname{free}(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}} P)\), canonical by
9273	  uniqueness of left adjoints.
9274	\end{proof}

9276	\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso commutes with sheafification]
9277	  \label{lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify}
9278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_commutes_sheafify}
9279	  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
9280	  \uses{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
9281	  With \(\varphi : X \cong Y\), \(\Phi\) and \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) as above, for every presheaf of
9282	  modules \(Q\) on \(X\) there is a natural isomorphism
9283	  \[
9284	    \Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(\operatorname{sheafification} Q\bigr)
9285	      \;\cong\;
9286	    \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}} Q\bigr).
9287	  \]
9288	\end{lemma}
9289	\begin{proof}
9290	  \uses{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
9291	  The homeomorphism underlying \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of sites between the opens of \(X\) and the
9292	  opens of \(Y\), under which covering sieves correspond. Hence the two sheafification adjunctions
9293	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}) correspond across
9294	  the equivalence: both \(\Phi \circ \operatorname{sheafification}\) and
9295	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \circ \Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) are left adjoint to the common composite of
9296	  the inclusion \(\iota\) with the presheaf transport \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}^{-1}\), so they agree up to
9297	  the canonical natural isomorphism of left adjoints.
9298	\end{proof}

9300	\begin{lemma}[The representable transports across the homeomorphism]
9301	  \label{lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
9302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
9303	  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
9304	  For a scheme isomorphism \(\varphi : X \cong Y\), whose underlying homeomorphism induces the
9305	  order-isomorphism of frames \(\operatorname{Opens} X \cong \operatorname{Opens} Y\), and any open
9306	  \(V \subseteq X\), the presheaf pushforward of the representable presheaf carries it to the
9307	  representable on the image open:
9308	  \[
9309	    \Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\, V\bigr) \;\cong\; \mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \,{''}\, V),
9310	  \]
9311	  where \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \,{''}\, V\) is the image open of \(V\) in \(Y\).
9312	\end{lemma}
9313	\begin{proof}
9314	  The presheaf pushforward \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) acts on opens by the inverse-image order-isomorphism
9315	  \(W \mapsto \varphi^{-1}(W)\) of the homeomorphism. Hence
9316	  \((\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\,\mathbf{y}\,V)(W) = (\mathbf{y}\,V)(\varphi^{-1} W)\) is inhabited exactly when
9317	  \(\varphi^{-1} W \subseteq V\), i.e.\ when \(W \subseteq \varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V\); this is
9318	  precisely \((\mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V))(W)\). The bijection is natural in \(W\)
9319	  because the open-image map is monotone and inverts the restriction order, so the two representables
9320	  coincide as presheaves.
9321	\end{proof}

9323	\begin{lemma}[A scheme isomorphism carries the corepresenting object along]
9324	  \label{lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso}
9325	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_transport_along_iso}
9326	  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
9327	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:pushforward_commutes_free, lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify,
9328	    lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
9329	  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes, \(V \subseteq X\) an open, and
9330	  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi : X.\mathrm{Modules} \simeq Y.\mathrm{Modules}\)
9331	  the induced equivalence. Then the pushforward carries the corepresenting object of
9332	  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou} along the isomorphism:
9333	  \[
9334	    \Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\bigr)
9335	      \;\cong\;
9336	    j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V},
9337	  \]
9338	  where \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V\) is the image open of \(V\) in \(Y\).
9339	\end{lemma}
9340	\begin{proof}
9341	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:pushforward_commutes_free, lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify,
9342	    lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
9343	  Unfold \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V = \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V))\)
9344	  (Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}) and chase the three commutations. Pushforward commutes with
9345	  sheafification (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify}):
9346	  \(\Phi(\operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V))) \cong
9347	  \operatorname{sheafification}(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)))\). It commutes
9348	  with the free functor (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_commutes_free}):
9349	  \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)) \cong
9350	  \operatorname{free}(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\mathbf{y}\,V))\). And the representable transports to the
9351	  representable on the image open (Lemma~\ref{lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}):
9352	  \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\mathbf{y}\,V) \cong \mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V)\). Composing the
9353	  three gives
9354	  \(\Phi(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong
9355	  \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V))) =
9356	  j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V}\).
9357	\end{proof}

9359	\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso preserves quasi-coherence]
9360	  \label{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
9361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
9362	  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
9363	  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and \(H : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a
9364	  quasi-coherent module. Then the pushforward \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) along
9365	  \(\varphi\) is again quasi-coherent.
9366	\end{lemma}
9367	\begin{proof}
9368	  Pushforward along the isomorphism of ringed spaces \(\varphi\) is the transport of module data along
9369	  the induced isomorphism of structure-ring sheaves \(X.\operatorname{ringCatSheaf} \cong
9370	  Y.\operatorname{ringCatSheaf}\): it relabels opens by the homeomorphism and the
9371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-action by the ring-sheaf isomorphism. Quasi-coherence is the local existence of a
9372	  presentation \(\mathcal{O}^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F} \to 0\) on a cover; such a
9373	  presentation transports verbatim across the iso of ringed spaces --- covers go to covers under the
9374	  homeomorphism and free modules to free modules under the ring-sheaf iso --- so \(\Phi H\) inherits a
9375	  local presentation and is quasi-coherent.
9376	\end{proof}

9398	  Let \(U\) be a scheme with \([\,\mathrm{IsAffine}\ U\,]\), let \(V \subseteq U\) be an affine open,
9399	  and let \(\mathcal{H}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. The whole-scheme isomorphism
